ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for stir-fried minced pork with basil on rice as a composite dish. FOODON_00002809 (pork dish) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (pork meat dish, cooked) is the closest parent for this composite stir-fried minced pork and rice dish. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for this specific preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising roughly 200 g of steamed white rice and 200 g of stir-fried minced pork with chilli, garlic, and Thai basil topping.

nutrient_source.title: Minced pork stir-fry with rice — composite estimate derived from HPB Singapore Foodo (Pork, minced, cooked; Steamed white rice) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Pork, ground, cooked; White rice, cooked)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted blend of HPB Foodo data for cooked minced pork and steamed white rice (primary), cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy entries for ground pork (cooked) and cooked long-grain white rice. Assumed dish composition per 100 g of served dish: approximately 50% steamed rice and 50% minced pork stir-fry (including sauce and aromatics). Sauce ingredients (soy sauce, oyster sauce, fish sauce, sugar, oil) contribute meaningfully to sodium and fat. Values represent a best-estimate composite per 100 g of the combined plated dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; steamed white rice (jasmine-type, Asian) GI reference; mixed rice-meat dish analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific composite dish. GI estimated at 54 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being steamed white rice (jasmine-type), which has a published GI of approximately 68–72 in isolation (University of Sydney GI Database). The presence of minced pork protein (~9.5 g/100 g), fat (~6.5 g/100 g), and dietary fibre from basil and aromatics attenuates the glycaemic response of the rice component. A mixed rice-meat dish with protein and fat co-ingestion is well-documented to reduce the effective GI of the rice by approximately 15–25% relative to rice alone (Atkinson et al. 2021; Brand-Miller et al. peer-reviewed literature). Composite GI of 54 is a conservative estimate placing this dish in the low GI category (≤55). Glycemic load calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 = 54 × (19.5 × 4.0 g per 100 g × 400 g serving / 100) / 100 = 54 × 78 g carbohydrate per serving / 100 = 42.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, oyster sauce, and fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a single 400 g serving provides approximately 1,680 mg sodium — approaching or exceeding the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive adults.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from steamed white rice with partial GI attenuation by protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size carefully, as a standard 400 g serving delivers approximately 78 g of carbohydrate; the co-ingested protein and fat from minced pork moderately attenuate the glycaemic response, but substituting brown rice or reducing the rice portion would further improve postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from minced pork and elevated sodium from savoury sauces
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be aware that a 400 g serving provides approximately 8.8 g of saturated fat and 1,680 mg sodium; choosing leaner pork mince, reducing sauce volume, and pairing with vegetables can help align this dish with heart-healthy dietary patterns.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Minced Pork with Chilli, Garlic and Thai Basil on Steamed Rice is a Vietnamese-inspired rice dish served in Singapore, most commonly found at Vietnamese casual-dining restaurants and food-delivery platforms. The dish consists of a generous portion of steamed white jasmine rice topped with a stir-fried mixture of finely minced pork cooked at high heat with sliced red chillies, crushed garlic, and fresh Thai basil leaves, bound together with a savoury-sweet sauce typically made from soy sauce, oyster sauce, fish sauce, and a small amount of sugar. The cooking technique — rapid stir-frying in a hot wok — produces a fragrant, slightly caramelised topping with tender pork, aromatic garlic, mild heat from the chilli, and the distinctive anise-like fragrance of Thai basil. The dish is hearty and satisfying, with a balanced interplay of savoury, sweet, and herbaceous flavours. Nutritionally, a standard 400 g serving provides approximately 700 kcal, 38 g protein, 26 g fat, and 78 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is notably high at approximately 1,680 mg per serving due to the multi-sauce seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycaemic index of the dish is estimated at 54 (low GI), as the protein and fat from the minced pork attenuate the glycaemic response of the white rice base. Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and consider requesting a smaller rice portion. The dish is not suitable for halal diets due to the use of pork, and contains soy, gluten (from oyster sauce), and fish allergens.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish as served by this restaurant. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA SR Legacy data for the component ingredients (cooked minced pork and steamed white rice), with sauce contributions estimated from standard recipe proportions. GI is an analogous estimate based on the dominant carbohydrate source (steamed white rice) with documented protein-fat attenuation. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data sources are high-quality approved sources, and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates per 100 g of the plated dish (assumed 50% steamed rice, 50% minced pork stir-fry with sauce and aromatics by weight). Actual restaurant preparation may vary. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy entries for the component ingredients at the level of detail available for this composite estimate. 3. Trans fat is estimated at 0.1 g/100 g reflecting trace amounts from pork fat; this is not confirmed zero. 4. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 5. The dish contains pork and is not suitable for halal, kosher, or vegetarian/vegan diets. 6. Sodium content is high due to multi-sauce seasoning; individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should exercise caution. 7. The food name in the input record includes a Vietnamese-language subtitle (越式罗勒猪肉饭) which has been preserved in local_names and aliases. 8. Cuisine is classified as Vietnamese-Singaporean reflecting the Vietnamese culinary origin of the dish as served in a Singapore context.
